headline: Chipmaker GlobalFoundries says CHIPS Act framework does not involve equity
summary: (Reuters) -GlobalFoundries said on Wednesday its CHIPS Act funding is "well intact" and the framework does not involve any type of equity, days after the U.S. government took a 10% equity stake in chipmaker Intel.  Speaking at the Deutsche Bank Technology Conference, the company's finance chief, John Hollister, said the company is receiving government funding under the act according to its milestone completion.  The government's move to take a 10% stake in Intel last week sparked questions about the outlook for corporate America after U.S. President Donald Trump said he plans to do other similar deals.

headline: Analysis: Investors worry Trump's Intel deal kicks off era of US industrial policy
summary: (Reuters) -The U.S. government's new stake in Intel is making some investors nervous that President Donald Trump's deal heralds an era of government meddling in private industry, particularly as the arrangement followed Trump's call for the resignation of the computer chip maker's CEO.  The deal, announced on Friday, converts $11.1 billion in Chips Act grants and other government funding into a 9.9% equity stake in Intel.  Trump had said on social media that Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan wanted to keep his job and "ended up giving us $10 billion for the United States."

headline: Nvidia earnings preview and the Trump chip tariff twist
summary: You can catch Trader Talk on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, or wherever you get your podcasts. Tariffs, AI, and Nvidia’s next report collide. They map the ripple effects across the market, from power-hungry data centers boosting utilities and energy to overlooked value in healthcare. Plus, the hardware sleepers to watch now include Cisco (CSCO) and IBM (IBM). Watch more episodes of Trader Talk here. Trader Talk with Kenny Polcari on Yahoo Finance delivers expert analysis and actionable insights, empowering you to navigate market volatility and secure your financial future. headline: US could seek piece of defense companies, Commerce chief says
summary: STORY: The Trump administration is considering taking stakes in defense companies and other firms it does business with, including Lockheed Martin and Boeing.That's according to U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, who told CNBC on Tuesday he believes talks are needed around how the government finances its munitions acquisitions, as he defended President Donald Trump's push for a greater government role in American business.Lutnick said Pentagon officials are considering taking pieces of government contractors, including AI powerhouse Palantir Technologies.Boeing declined to comment, and representatives from Lockheed and Palantir did not immediately respond to a request for comment.Lutnick's comments came a day after President Trump said he wants to make more government investments in American companies...having already announced a near-10% stake in Intel and revenue-sharing deals with fellow chipmakers Nvidia and AMD.But critics warn this change could potentially impede these companies' independence, said Liz Miller, president of Summit Place Financial Advisors."If President Trump starts this, and promises no interaction, that doesn't tell us at all what future presidents might do."Shares of Lockheed Martin, Boeing and Palantir Technologies were all higher in Tuesday morning trading.

headline: Wall St futures slip ahead of Walmart's results, Fed meet

The selloff could also be a result of investors paring back their stock exposure during a traditionally rocky period for equities, according to the Stock Trader's Almanac.  "Equities could be more at risk of volatility amid this week's selloff in AI-related stocks on the back of renewed doubts about AI valuations," said Raffi Boyadjian, lead market analyst at brokerage XM.
